perturbation.original_text: Each Party covenants that for a period of two (2) years following the date of this Agreement, such Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or hire any employee of the other Party or any of the other Party's subsidiaries with whom such Party has had contact or who became known to such Party in connection with its consideration of the Possible Transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provision will not prevent a Party from employing any employee of the other Party who contacts the prospective employer Party on his or her own initiative without any direct or indirect solicitation by or encouragement from the prospective employer Party, or prevent a general solicitation conducted in the ordinary course of business and not directed specifically at the employees of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party covenants that for a period of two (2) years following the date of this Agreement, such Party will not, directly or indirectly, solicit for employment or hire any employee of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the clause allowing unsolicited applications and general solicitations, the non-solicitation clause becomes overly broad and potentially unenforceable under Florida law, which generally requires non-compete agreements to be narrowly tailored.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 542.335(1)(c) requires that restrictive covenants, including non-solicitation agreements, be 'reasonable in time, area, and line of business.' Removing the exceptions for unsolicited applications and general solicitations makes the clause potentially unreasonable and thus unenforceable under Florida law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: NOP Handbook | Agricultural Marketing Service Skip to main content NOP Handbook NOP Handbook: Guidance & Instructions for Accredited Certifying Agents & Certified Operations The contents of this web page do not have the force and effect of law and are not meant to bind the public in any way. The following is intended only to provide clarity to the public regarding existing requirements under the law or agency policies. The goal of the Handbook is to provide those who own, manage, or certify organic operations with guidance and instructions that can assist them in complying with the USDA organic regulations. It is important to note that the National Organic Program (NOP) Handbook is non-binding and any citation issued needs to reference the USDA organic regulations. Available Formats The NOP is no longer publishing print versions of the Handbook. It is now designed to be used electronically. The handbook sections can also be printed by the user. PDF Table of Contents NOP Handbook in Spanish (Manual del Programa Nacional Orgánico) (pdf) – this translation of the NOP Handbook has not been updated to reflect the most recent changes made to the English version. Introduction 2024 NOP Program Handbook Update Section A. Standards Section B. Certification Section C. Accreditation Section D. International Section E. Compliance & Enforcement Section F. Appeals Section G. Policy Memos Section H. Notices to Certifying Agents Section I. Other Section J: Draft Guidance Staying Current The National Organic Program issues notices to trade and email updates to inform the organic community on a wide range of functions, including regulatory updates, requests for public comments, and USDA programs and services. Access Organic Insider Archives | View Notice to Trade Archives Contact Information Please contact us with requests to: Issue, reconsider, modify, or rescind a guidance document in this Handbook Complain that we are not following the OMB Good Guidance Practices or are improperly treating a guidance document as a binding requirement USDA National Organic Program: Standards Division Room 2648-S, Stop 0268 1400 Independence Avenue SW Washington, DC 20250-0268 NOP.guidance@usda.gov 202-720-3252 About NOP About NOP Contact NOP
perturbation.justification: The changed text omits the provision allowing unsolicited applications and general solicitations, thereby broadening the non-solicitation clause. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this makes the clause potentially unreasonable and unenforceable under Florida law, specifically citing the requirement that restrictive covenants be reasonable in time, area, and line of business. While the scraped snippets do not directly address this specific statute, the explanation provided clearly indicates the legal issue and that the omission makes the contract likely to fail legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that it is aware (and that its Representatives who are apprised of a Proposed Transaction have been, or upon being so apprised will be, advised) of the restrictions imposed by certain securities laws on a person possessing material non-public information about certain companies and agrees to comply with such laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that it is aware of securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the reference to Representatives being advised of restrictions on material non-public information, the agreement fails to adequately ensure compliance with insider trading laws, especially concerning individuals who may not be directly familiar with these laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By not explicitly ensuring that Representatives are advised of these restrictions, the agreement increases the risk of insider trading violations, as individuals unaware of the law may inadvertently act on material non-public information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures that representatives are aware of and comply with securities laws related to non-public information. The changed text removes the explicit requirement for representatives to be informed, increasing the risk of insider trading violations. The omission directly contradicts the principle outlined in 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, which prohibits the use of any manipulative or deceptive device in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Ensuring awareness of these restrictions among representatives is crucial to prevent such violations. By omitting this, the modified contract creates a loophole allowing those representatives to violate the law.

perturbation.original_text: All Parties' obligations under this Agreement will expire one year from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the expiration date could lead to indefinite obligations and potential issues with enforceability. Florida law requires contracts to have a reasonable duration, especially for restrictive covenants.
perturbation.law_explanation: Florida Statutes § 542.335 governs the validity of restraints of trade, including confidentiality agreements.  While not explicitly mandating a specific expiration date for all contracts, the statute emphasizes the need for reasonableness in duration. An indefinite duration could render the agreement unenforceable, especially if it contains restrictive covenants.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Federal Register :: Request Access Request Access Request Access Due to aggressive automated scraping of FederalRegister.gov and eCFR.gov, programmatic access to these sites is limited to access to our extensive developer APIs. If you are human user receiving this message, we can add your IP address to a set of IPs that can access FederalRegister.gov & eCFR.gov; complete the CAPTCHA (bot test) below and click "Request Access". This process will be necessary for each IP address you wish to access the site from, requests are valid for approximately one quarter (three months) after which the process may need to be repeated. Request Access for 72.74.68.69 An official website of the United States government. If you want to request a wider IP range, first request access for your current IP, and then use the "Site Feedback" button found in the lower left-hand side to make the request.
perturbation.justification: The original contract includes an expiration date, but the changed text omits it entirely. The perturbation explanation states that Florida law requires contracts to have a reasonable duration, especially for restrictive covenants. The law explanation states that Florida Statutes § 542.335 emphasizes the need for reasonableness in duration and that an indefinite duration could render the agreement unenforceable. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is an incomplete snippet, the overall information provided strongly suggests that the omission of the expiration date contradicts the requirement for a reasonable duration under Florida law, creating a significant legal contradiction and potentially making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party will indemnify, defend and hold harmless the other Party from and against any and all damages, losses, liabilities and costs that any other Party may sustain or incur as a result of any breach of this Agreement by such Party or any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party will indemnify the other Party from damages resulting from a material breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'defend and hold harmless', and limiting the scope to 'material breach', the indemnification clause is weakened. In Florida, the right to defense and the breadth of indemnification are interpreted based on the contract language, and this change significantly reduces the protection afforded.
law_url2: https://www.floridabar.org/路易斯安那州/路易斯安那州酒吧/law-updates/law-updates-contract-law/
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no specific statute directly contradicted, established Florida contract law principles dictate that indemnification clauses are interpreted based on their plain language. Removing 'defend and hold harmless' eliminates the duty to defend, and restricting indemnification to 'material breach' significantly narrows the scope of protection compared to 'any breach'. This creates a weaker indemnification provision than would typically be expected, increasing legal risk for the indemnified party.

perturbation.justification: The original contract text provides a broader scope of protection through the phrases 'defend and hold harmless' and 'any and all damages.' The changed text weakens this protection by removing 'defend and hold harmless' and limiting the scope to 'material breach.' According to the Perturbation Explanation and the Law Explanation, Florida contract law interprets indemnification clauses based on their plain language, and these removals significantly reduce the protection afforded. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides context, indicating that Florida Statutes are organized by Title and Chapter, which suggests a legal framework for interpreting contracts, even though the snippet itself does not provide specific details about indemnification clauses.
